questions.question: How do compound components share state between parent and child components?
options: Through a Pinia store that both parent and child import
Via `provide` in the parent and `inject` in the child components
Through a global event bus attached to `app.config.globalProperties`
By passing all state down as props through every intermediate component

questions.question: What is the advantage of using an `InjectionKey` symbol instead of a string key in provide/inject?
options: Symbols are faster to look up than strings at runtime
It provides type safety and avoids naming collisions between different injection keys
Symbols prevent child components from accidentally overwriting the injected value
It is required by Vue when using TypeScript strict mode

questions.question: When is the compound components pattern a better choice than a props-only approach?
options: When the component only needs one or two simple configuration options
When the parent and children are in different modules of the application
When children have complex content like sub-components or conditional logic that doesn't fit in a data array
When the component library needs to be published as an npm package
